EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A continental air mass moved over Florida the weekend of January 7-8, with the parent high pressure system moving directly over the Florida peninsula on the night/early morning of the 7th and 8th.  This created ideal radiational cooling conditions, and temperatures dropped to at or below freezing over interior sections of South Florida during the pre-dawn and early morning hours of the 8th.  Minimum temperatures were as low as 29 degrees at the Florida Agricultural Weather Network (FAWN) station in Belle Glade.  Other minimum temperature readings were: 30 at Palmdale and 31 at Immokalee, Ortona, and Devils Garden. Temperatures were at or below freezing for 2 to 4 hours at some locations. This caused some crop damage to approximately 410,000 acres in Western Palm Beach County. Roughly 1000 farms and nurseries were affected.  Damage was particularly severe to the snap bean crop, with estimates of 80-90 percent losses. The sweet corn crop also suffered significantly, with about 25 to 50 percent loss.

A weak tornado touched down along State Highway 4 in Baker near the Post Office.  The tornado tracked northeast and tore part of the roof off the Baker School.  The winds from the storm funneled into part of the school and peeled sections of the roof off.  Kids inside quickly got underneath their desks.  Water from the rain fell into the classrooms and caused part of the ceiling to collapse.  Thirteen kids suffered minor injuries.   At least four classrooms at the school were damaged.   The weak tornado continued tracking northeast and damaged several more homes before lifting back into the clouds near Old River Road.

An F1 tornado touched down near the intersection of County Road 178 and State Highway 87.  Several manufactured homes were damaged where the tornado first touched down.  The tornado tracked northeast and crossed Highway 87 and caused only minor damage east of the highway.  Near Highway 87 a home had most of the roof torn off.  The roof of the home had reportedly been damaged, twice, by  high winds associated with hurricanes Ivan and Dennis over the past year and a half.  Both times the roof was repaired.  No injuries were reported.
High winds from a thunderstorm blew down several trees and power lines near Belleview.

The combination of tropical moisture flowing into a line of thunderstorms and an approaching upper level disturbance allowed a train of intense thunderstorms to repeatedly cross over parts of the Tampa Bay area. Between 8 and more than 11 inches of rain fell in roughly a five hour period in a five mile wide stripe extending from Madeira Beach northeast through Pinellas Park, then across Old Tampa Bay to west Tampa, including Tampa International Airport. The area of heaviest rain was so concentrated that downtown St. Petersburg, less than 10 miles away, recorded less than an inch of rain during the same period.  The torrential rains caused flash flooding in the areas where more than 8 inches fell. The flash flooding prompted the mayor of St. Petersburg to term the event a "hundred year flood".  In Lealman, an entire mobile home community was evacuated, and at least 60 of the homes were flooded. A partial roof collapse was reported at a big box store in St. Petersburg.  Water pouring into the store washed out several cash register stands and injured one employee as they were washed into the parking lot.  Another roof collapsed at Treasure Island. Hundreds of vehicles were stranded by the flood waters. Arctic high pressure settled over Florida on February 13th, and set the stage for a significant freeze and frost event over interior sections of South Florida overnight into early morning on the 14th.  This was the second freeze to affect this area in 2006, the first being on January 8th. Lowest official temperature readings were 28 at Immokalee, 29 at Palmdale, Belle Glade and Labelle, 30 at Ortona and Clewiston and 31 at South Bay and Big Cypress. Naples Municipal Airport reported a low of 34 degrees, 1 degree shy of a record for the date, and the Homestead Extension Office reported a low of 35 degrees at 2 ft. Unofficial readings from farms outside of Belle Glade registered temperatures in the mid 20s. Many of these temperature readings were the coldest since January 5th, 2001, with a few locations being the coldest since January 24th, 2003. Damage to crops was extensive, particularly to the corn and green bean crop in Western Palm Beach County. Estimated losses in this area were in the 80 to 90 percent range for green beans and 70 to 80 percent range for corn. Leafy vegetable crop damage was less, estimated at 15 to 30 percent. The corn and green bean crop is estimated to be worth at least $50 million. This is the second devastating freeze to the corn and bean crop in Western Palm Beach County in 2006, which followed damage caused by Hurricane Wilma in October 2005. The citrus crop in Southwest Florida fared quite well with no significant damage noted. Some damage to tomato crops were noted in Immokalee, but was not widespread.

Frequent lightning from a line of strong to severe thunderstorms caused a fire that destroyed a newly-refurbished World War II barracks on the Florida Atlantic University campus.  The barracks, built almost entirely of Dade County pine, crumbled under the fire within minutes. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The center of Tropical Storm Alberto made landfall near Adams Beach along the Taylor County coast, about 50 miles southeast of Tallahassee, Florida, near midday on June 13.  The highest 2-minute wind measured was 36 knots at Keaton Beach around 3 pm EDT on June 13.  A peak wind gust of 48 knots was recorded from Gulf Buoy 42036, about 80 miles southeast of Apalachicola.  It also recorded the lowest sea level pressure,  995.2 millibars.  Rainfall totals for the 48-hour period ending at 8 am EDT on June 14 ranged from 0.26 inches at Marianna to 7.90 inches at Ochlockonee Bay.  Maximum storm tides from Alberto varied from 1-2 feet above MLLW along the Bay and Gulf County coasts to 8-9 feet above MLLW along the Taylor and Dixie County coasts.  Maximum storm surges ranged from 3-5 feet above normal along the Wakulla, Taylor and Dixie County coasts.  Alberto's winds knocked down several trees and power lines throughout portions of the Florida Big Bend and resulted in scattered power outages.  Minor surge flooding occurred in coastal communities, especially in Taylor and Dixie counties.  About 350 coastal residents of Franklin, Wakulla, Taylor and Dixie counties took refuge in emergency shelters.  Reports were provided by the Florida DEM.

Tropical Storm "Alberto" Tropical Storm Alberto made landfall along the northeast Gulf of Mexico coast and as a weakening system move north-northeast across north Florida and southeast Georgia. This motion positioned the area on the north and east side of the storm. Northeast Florida experienced periods of sustained tropical storm force winds and gusts. Heavy rainfall occurred across the Suwannee River Valley. The lowest mean sea level pressure recorded in northeast Florida was 1006.1 mb measured in Gainesville  as the center moved north northwest of the city on June 13th around 11:53Z.  The lowest mean sea level readings across northeast Florida ranged from 1006.1 mb in Gainesville to 1009.4 mb at the NOS tide gauge located at Mayport Naval Station in east Jacksonville. Maximum sustained winds recorded were 40 knots/46 mph at Craig Airfield in east Jacksonville on June 13th around 21:11Z. Stations generally reported sustained winds of 20-40 knots (23-46 mph).  The highest wind gust was recorded at Cecil Field in west Jacksonville on June 13th around 19:50Z. Scattered trees and power lines were blown down across the area. Most wind damage occurred from midday June 13th through the evening of June 13th. Wave heights reached 6-9 ft across the coastal waters.  The maximum height observed was at the St. Augustine buoy 41012 on June 13th around 1920Z.  Storm tides ran 0.5-1 ft below predicted tide levels at Fernandina Beach (NOS) and Mayport (NOS).   Rainfall totals across northeast Florida ranged from 2 to 6 inches. The heaviest rainfall 5.60 inches was recorded in Hedges, Nassau County. Some minor temporary flooding of flood prone occurred, but the rainfall was beneficial to the area helping it recover from a minor drought period.  River flooding was not a concern. Two confirmed tornado touchdowns occurred, one near Jacksonville Beach on June 13th around 08:00Z. Minor damage to a water park was reported. No fatalities nor injuries were reported in association with T.S. Alberto.  Minor problems from wind gusts with scattered power outages occurred, as well as minor temporary flooding.

A 52-year old male drowned in a rip current trying to save his wife at St. Augustine Beach.
Jacksonville International Airport ASOS (KJAX) measured 1.83 inches of rainfall in one hour.
Parts of St. Joe Plaza near the Palm Coast Parkway were under two feet of water. A vehicle stalled out from being partially submerged.

Trees were uprooted across the eastside of Jacksonville due to strong thunderstorm winds. One tree was uprooted in the Raleigh Creek Subdivision in Fort Caroline around 230 pm LDT. Two large oaks were blown down and a fence damaged at a residence in east Arlington along Berry Road, near Arlington Road around 240 pm LDT.  The final report of wind damage associated with this storm was at Jacksonville Beach around 3 pm LDT where 3 trees were blown down. One of these trees fell on a residence at the 500 block of 12th Avenue South.
A large tree was leaning over and almost touching the ground at the Kernan Blvd exit on J.T. Butler Blvd. The time is approximate.

Seven people were injured when a large oak tree fell on a car that was stopped at a traffic light at Penman and Florida Roads. The people were taken to the hospital. The time of the event is approximate.

The Briar Patch Farm at 8850 NW 193rd Street in Orange Lake had 3 large oak trees blown over, a third of a barn roof destroyed and some roof damage to a house and outside sheds. The time is estimated based on radar.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A peak wind gust of 70 mph was recorded at the Panama City-Bay County Airport.  Several small planes suffered major damage.  Some of these planes were torn from their tie downs and tossed into hangars and each other.  One plane was flipped over.  One hangar door was removed and lofted into the air and traveled about 100 yards.  A Canadair regional jet was moved 15 feet back from a jetway at the terminal.  Damage survey reported by an NWS employee.

A 20-year-old male died of apparent heat exposure after he wandered in the Everglades in search of help to repair his broken-down airboat. He and his uncle set out on the airboat from Loxahatchee National Wildlife Refuge in southern Palm Beach County, and were about 3 miles south of the ramp in extreme northern Broward County when the airboat got stuck in the mud. High temperatures in the area that day were in the lower 90s and lowest relative humidity values were around 55 percent, producing maximum heat index values of near 105 degrees.
A woman carrying a child outside was struck by lightning. The child was uninjured. The woman was transported to a hospital in critical condition.
A 10 year old female was struck by lightning while outside. She sustained minor injuries.

A 51 year old male was hospitalized after lightning struck his vehicle while he was waiting for a train.  He had his arm out an open window and was tapping fingers on door when he was struck. The lightning may have hit the train first, then the vehicle.  A second vehicle behind the first was also struck and a women was transported to the hospital for her injuries.
An 11-year-old boy died of heat stroke during football practice.  A 12-year-old boy died a few days earlier during football practice but the cause was undetermined.

Lightning struck a home near the South Florida Fairgrounds, causing a fire in the attic. No one was injured.  Approximately 400 lightning strikes were noted in central Palm Beach County between 7 and 9 PM.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow moving thunderstorm produced numerous reports of funnel clouds along the Polk and Hillsborough County Line.
A slow moving thunderstorm produced numerous reports of funnel clouds along the Polk and Hillsborough County Line.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A scoreboard at the Tampa Catholic High School was blown down and the weather station at the school reported a 61 MPH wind gust.
A small tornado briefly touched down in a field in a rural area near State Road 70 and the Manatee-DeSoto County line.
Thunderstorm winds downed power lines lines across four homes near the intersection of South Tuttle Avenue and Woods Street.
An amateur radio operator reported nickel size hail in Port Charlotte.
The public reported a funnel cloud near Mims.
One inch hail was reported in Barefoot Bay.
Television news media reported a brief tornado touchdown in Apopka, bringing down a few trees and power lines.
Nickel size hail was reported west of Chiefland near the Suwannee River.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Tropical Storm Alberto became a storm north of the Yucatan Peninsula on June 11 and made landfall in the Big Bend area of Florida on June 13.  The center of Tropical Storm Alberto moved from about 350 miles west of Fort Myers on the morning of August 12 to about 80 miles west of Cedar Key on the morning of August 13. Alberto brought widespread rains of 2 to 5 inches with isolated 7 inches to west central and southwest Florida. In addition, Alberto created at least four tornadoes and a storm surge of 2 to 4 feet. There were two  indirect fatalities related to the storm. Two men died near Egmont Key on June 10 during a storm in one of Alberto's rain bands. A woman fell overboard when the band of showers and surging currents made navigation difficult. Her husband and a friend jumped in to save her but they did not have life jackets and drowned. The woman made it back to the boat and was saved. In Levy County...a peak wind gust from the south of 48 knots (55 MPH) was recorded at 5 AM EDT on the 13th at the C-MAN station on Cedar Key.  The storm tide at Cedar Key was 6.74 feet which included a surge of 4.09 feet and occurred at 8:30 AM on the 13th.  Two feet of water covered the roads in downtown Yankeetown and near shore areas of Highways 40 and 24.  About 20 homes received minor damage in the Yankeetown area, however, these homes are in a low area and are susceptible to unusually high tides as well. Cedar Key did not experience flooded roads as the tide was going down as the surge was coming in. In Citrus County...a peak wind gust from the southwest at an estimated 42 knots (48 MPH) at 12:30 PM EDT on the 13th at the Crystal River Nuclear Power Plant.  The storm tide at the plant was 7.60 feet which included a surge of 3.70 feet and occurred around 1 PM EDT.  Storm surge flooding near Homossassa put 3 feet of water into one restaurant and damaged 20 homes.  Two people who did not evacuate had to be rescued.  Fort Island Trail was covered with 4 feet of water at times.  In Hernando County...a peak wind gust from the southwest of 42 knots (48 MPH) was recorded around 7 AM EDT at the COMPS station at Homosassa.  The storm surge was estimated at 4 feet and approached homes in the Hernando Beach area, but the homes remained dry.  The ASOS station at the Brooksville Airport (KBKV) recorded the highest rainfall total of 2.60 inches. In Sumter County...a peak wind gust from the southwest of 36 knots (41 MPH) was recorded at 12:05 PM EDT at the AWOS station at The Villages (KVVG).  Rainfall in Lake Panosoffkee was 1.85 inches.In Pasco County...a peak wind gust from the southwest of 47 knots (54 MPH) around 5 AM EDT was recorded at the COMPS station at the Fred Howard Park.  The storm surge was estimated at 4 feet up and down the coast line but it was not enough to cause property damage. Rainfall in Saint Leo was 2.62 inches. In Pinellas County...a peak wind gust from the southwest of 53 knots (61 MPH) around 8 AM EDT was recorded at the C-MAN station on Clearwater Beach (CWBF1).  The storm surge was 2.42 feet at Clearwater Beach and 2.52 feet in Saint Petersburg.  Water covered the roads in Shore Acres in northeast Saint Petersburg due to surge and fresh water flooding. In Hillsborough County...a peak wind gust from the southwest of 49 knots (56 MPH) at 2:37 AM EDT was recorded at the MacDill Air Force Base (KMCF).  The storm surge was 3.28 feet at McKay Bay and 2.75 feet at the Old Port Tampa COMPS station. The overspray from the surge combined with fresh water flooding closed parts of Bay Shore Blvd.  In Manatee County...a peak wind gust of 38 knots (44 MPH) at 2:39 AM EDT was recorded at the Bradenton/Sarasota International Airport (KSRQ).  The storm surge was 2.06 feet at Port Manatee. In Sarasota County...a peak wind gust of 46 knots (53 MPH) at 2:37 AM was recorded at the C-MAN station in Venice (VENF1). Rainfall in Myakka State Park totalled 3.34 inches.

In Pinellas County...a peak wind gust from the southwest of 53 knots (61 MPH) around 8 AM EDT was recorded at the C-MAN station on Clearwater Beach (CWBF1).  The storm surge was 2.42 feet at Clearwater Beach and 2.52 feet in Saint Petersburg.  Water covered the roads in Shore Acres in northeast Saint Petersburg due to surge and fresh water flooding. In Hillsborough County...a peak wind gust from the southwest of 49 knots (56 MPH) at 2:37 AM EDT was recorded at the MacDill Air Force Base (KMCF).  The storm surge was 3.28 feet at McKay Bay and 2.75 feet at the Old Port Tampa COMPS station. The overspray from the surge combined with fresh water flooding closed parts of Bay Shore Blvd.  In Manatee County...a peak wind gust of 38 knots (44 MPH) at 2:39 AM EDT was recorded at the Bradenton/Sarasota International Airport (KSRQ).  The storm surge was 2.06 feet at Port Manatee. In Sarasota County...a peak wind gust of 46 knots (53 MPH) at 2:37 AM was recorded at the C-MAN station in Venice (VENF1). Rainfall in Myakka State Park totalled 3.34 inches.

Tropical Storm Alberto became a storm north of the Yucatan Peninsula on June 11 and made landfall in the Big Bend area of Florida on June 13.  The center of Tropical Storm Alberto moved from about 350 miles west of Fort Myers on the morning of August 12 to about 80 miles west of Cedar Key on the morning of August 13. Alberto brought widespread rains of 2 to 5 inches with isolated 7 inches to west central and southwest Florida. In addition, Alberto created at least four tornadoes and a storm surge of 2 to 4 feet. There were two  indirect fatalities related to the storm. Two men died near Egmont Key on June 10 during a storm in one of Alberto's rain bands. A woman fell overboard when the band of showers and surging currents made navigation difficult. Her husband and a friend jumped in to save her but they did not have life jackets and drowned. The woman made it back to the boat and was saved. In Levy County...a peak wind gust from the south of 48 knots (55 MPH) was recorded at 5 AM EDT on the 13th at the C-MAN station on Cedar Key.  The storm tide at Cedar Key was 6.74 feet which included a surge of 4.09 feet and occurred at 8:30 AM on the 13th.  Two feet of water covered the roads in downtown Yankeetown and near shore areas of Highways 40 and 24.  About 20 homes received minor damage in the Yankeetown area, however, these homes are in a low area and are susceptible to unusually high tides as well. Cedar Key did not experience flooded roads as the tide was going down as the surge was coming in. In Citrus County...a peak wind gust from the southwest at an estimated 42 knots (48 MPH) at 12:30 PM EDT on the 13th at the Crystal River Nuclear Power Plant.  The storm tide at the plant was 7.60 feet which included a surge of 3.70 feet and occurred around 1 PM EDT.  Storm surge flooding near Homossassa put 3 feet of water into one restaurant and damaged 20 homes.  Two people who did not evacuate had to be rescued.  Fort Island Trail was covered with 4 feet of water at times.  In Hernando County...a peak wind gust from the southwest of 42 knots (48 MPH) was recorded around 7 AM EDT at the COMPS station at Homosassa.  The storm surge was estimated at 4 feet and approached homes in the Hernando Beach area, but the homes remained dry.  The ASOS station at the Brooksville Airport (KBKV) recorded the highest rainfall total of 2.60 inches. In Sumter County...a peak wind gust from the southwest of 36 knots (41 MPH) was recorded at 12:05 PM EDT at the AWOS station at The Villages (KVVG).  Rainfall in Lake Panosoffkee was 1.85 inches.In Pasco County...a peak wind gust from the southwest of 47 knots (54 MPH) around 5 AM EDT was recorded at the COMPS station at the Fred Howard Park.  The storm surge was estimated at 4 feet up and down the coast line but it was not enough to cause property damage. Rainfall in Saint Leo was 2.62 inches. In Pinellas County...a peak wind gust from the southwest of 53 knots (61 MPH) around 8 AM EDT was recorded at the C-MAN station on Clearwater Beach (CWBF1).  The storm surge was 2.42 feet at Clearwater Beach and 2.52 feet in Saint Petersburg.  Water covered the roads in Shore Acres in northeast Saint Petersburg due to surge and fresh water flooding. In Hillsborough County...a peak wind gust from the southwest of 49 knots (56 MPH) at 2:37 AM EDT was recorded at the MacDill Air Force Base (KMCF).  The storm surge was 3.28 feet at McKay Bay and 2.75 feet at the Old Port Tampa COMPS station. The overspray from the surge combined with fresh water flooding closed parts of Bay Shore Blvd.  In Manatee County...a peak wind gust of 38 knots (44 MPH) at 2:39 AM EDT was recorded at the Bradenton/Sarasota International Airport (KSRQ).  The storm surge was 2.06 feet at Port Manatee. In Sarasota County...a peak wind gust of 46 knots (53 MPH) at 2:37 AM was recorded at the C-MAN station in Venice (VENF1). Rainfall in Myakka State Park totalled 3.34 inches.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The rainfall was 4.56 inches.
The rainfall was 3.86 inches.
Trained spotters measured 60 MPH winds with their home weather stations.
Trained spotters reported a path of penny sized hail from west of Brooksville to Spring Hill.
The rainfall was 4.15 inches.
The rainfall was 6.76 inches at the National Weather Service office 3 miles east of Ruskin.  Elsewhere the rainfall was widespread at 3.5 to 5 inches throughout the county.
The rainfall was 5.57 inches in Bradenton.  Elsewhere rainfall was widespread at 3 to 5 inches throughout the county.
The highest rainfall report during Tropical Storm Alberto was 7.08 inches five miles east of Tarpon Springs.  Elsewhere, widespread 3 to 5 inches of rain was reported across the county.
The rainfall was 5.69 inches in the Four Corners area.  Rainfall reports of 3 to 5 inches were common in the west half of Polk County.
The public reported a funnel cloud north of Satellite Beach.
A male construction worker in his 30s was transported to the hospital after he was struck by lightning near Interstate 75 and Bruce B. Downs Blvd.
Emergency Management relayed a report from the public of a brief tornado touch down in an open field with no damage.
A trained spotter reported nickel size hail at the intersection of Dale Mabry Highway and Hudson Lane.
A funnel cloud was reported near Deer Park.
NASA reported over 4 inches of rain over the Kennedy Space Center complex.
Lightning started a fire that destroyed a $2 million house.

A waterspout touched down in the St. Johns River along Highway 44 east of Paisley. It quickly made landfall near a marina on the Lake County side of the river. It destroyed a pontoon boat and severly damaged eight others.
The Tampa Police Department reported downed tree limbs and power lines near Armenia and Interstate 275.
Trained spotter reported small to medium size tree branches broken.
The wind gust was measured by a COMPS station on the island.
A spotter estimated wind gusts to 52 knots in Holly Hill.
A lightning strike caused a fire that severly damaged a house in Apopka.
A 42 year old man drowned while assisting in the rescue of two young boys caught in a rip current.

The Gulf Harbor Yacht Club recorded 5.58 inches of rain that lead to minor urban flooding. Other reports included 4.50 inches near Tanglewood and Whiskey Creek, and 3.42 inches at a marina in Cape Coral.  A narrow band of heavy rain was recorded in this area by the Doppler radar in Ruskin, FL.
A single funnel cloud was reported from numerous locations, including Palmetto, Bradenton, and Ellenton.
Lee County Emergency Management reported numerous cars stalled in flooded streets in Cape Coral and Fort Myers.  Water entered several homes in Fort Myers near Dean Park.
Hail up to one inch deep was reported at three locations around Ft. Pierce.
A funnel cloud was reported west of Hobe Sound.

Sixteen single family homes were damaged by flying debris and reverberations caused by an explosion of a 40 foot decaying pine tree due to a lightning strike. The expanding steam and resin from the wet but dead tree created a one block debris field. The explosion from the strike shattered windows, lifted roofs, caved ceilings, dented cars, and bent fences. A 14-foot long trench about a half a foot deep was formed as the roots exploded. In all, 29 windows were broken and 2 homes reported damage of $30,000 or more. One man was treated for minor injuries.

A severe thunderstorm affected Northwestern Collier County in the area of Immokalee Road 1-2 miles east of Interstate 75.  Damage was done to a lanai screen cage, and several small trees and pine tree limbs were reported downed in the area.
Waterspouts witnessed by several people came onshore near the Deerfield Beach Pier as a tornado. The tornado lasted about 1 minute before dissipating. No damage was reported.
General public observed a funnel cloud in the vicinity of the Florida Turnpike in Hollywood.  The observer was located in eastern Hollywood and estimated the funnel cloud to be about 6 miles west.

A 34-year-old woman was struck by lightning just after stepping out of her car. She was holding an umbrella.  The victim was not breathing when paramedics arrived, but she was revived on the trip to the hospital.Strong thunderstorms were observed in the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Tropical Storm Ernesto moved across the Straits of Florida during the morning and early afternoon hours of August 29th, making landfall in the Upper Florida Keys around sunset, with a second landfall over far southwestern Miami-Dade County around midnight on the 30th. Ernesto moved north-northwest over the Everglades of South Florida during the overnight and morning hours of the 30th, then turned north and northeast over eastern sections of Hendry County during the midday, and passing over western portions of Lake Okeechobee during the early afternoon hours of the 30th. Ernesto is characterized as a minor tropical storm with little overall impact on South Florida, and no direct casualties were noted. There were two indirect deaths in Miami-Dade and Broward counties resulting from vehicle accidents.Maximum observed sustained winds were 42 knots (48 mph) at the C-MAN station in Fowey Rocks, with a peak gust of 50 knots (58 mph).  Due to the higher than standard anemometer height at Fowey Rocks, a reduction to standard 10-meter elevation yielded a highest sustained wind of 37 knots (43 mph).  Tropical storm force winds were confined to coastal sections along the Atlantic coast, as well as over Lake Okeechobee where South Florida Water Management District platforms LZ40 and L005 recorded maximum sustained winds of 35 and 34 knots (40 and 39 mph) respectively. Platform L005 recorded a peak gust of 51 knots (59 mph). The lowest sea-level pressure observed was 1004 mb in Opa-Locka, although estimated lowest pressures on Lake Okeechobee were 1003 mb. Coastal storm tides of around 1 foot above astronomical tide levels were observed on both Atlantic and Gulf coasts, with no beach erosion reported. Rainfall was heaviest over interior sections of South Florida, primarily over the Big Cypress National Preserve of Collier County where 48-hour storm total amounts ranged between 7 and 8 inches. Other rainfall amounts in excess of 5 inches were observed over the rest of interior South Florida including the areas around Lake Okeechobee. These rains caused the Fisheating Creek to overflow its banks at Palmdale in Glades County, and also resulted in some evacuation of homes. Flooding also occurred in portions of Collier County, especially near Golden Gate Estates and Immokalee, with no damage to structures reported.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A rain band from Tropical Storm Ernesto produced a narrow swath of 3 to 5 inches in eastern Lee County.  Rainfall of 4.38 inches was recorded in Lehigh Acres. Minor flooding was reported near Tackahoe Road Near Bedman Creek in Alva and several roads in Lehigh Acres. A second narrow band of heavy rain fell from Captiva Island to south of Fort Myers Beach. Rainfall of 3.28 inches was recorded at Lovers Key.  Tides rose to one foot above normal well after Ernesto had passed.
Lightning sparked a fire that destroyed a home on the 700 block of Shorecrest Drive.

A nearby lightning strike injured four plumbers as they were preparing to seek shelter from an approaching storm. One of the men were knocked unconscious and another could not feel his legs for about an hour. The men were taken to the hospital and released.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lightning ignited a fire that destroyed a home on the 1500 block of SW 29th Terrace. The owners were not home at the time and the hurricane shutters had to be cut off the home so fire fighters could battle the blaze.  Damage was estimated at $185,000 for the structure and $15,000 for the contents.

A rain band from Tropical Storm Ernesto produced a narrow swath of 2 to 4 inches of rain along Interstate 75 in Charlotte County.  Rainfall reports from the the hardest hit areas in south central parts of the county were not available but were estimated by radar.  The Charlotte County Airport reported 2.27 inches of rain.  Tides rose to one foot above normal well after Ernesto had passed.
Quarter size hail was reported near the high school at the intersection of State Road 54 and U.S. Highway 301.
Nickel size hail was reported in the Meadow Pointe subdivision near the intersection of Meadow Pointe Blvd. and Bruce B. Downs Blvd.
Local emergency management officials reported a funnel cloud over Lake Griffin.

Flash flooding from very heavy rains associated with Tropical Storm Ernesto affected inland portions of South Florida on August 30th. Hardest hit was the Palmdale area in Glades County where an estimated 3 to 5 inches of water entered at least 13 homes, necessitating the evacuation of residents. In Immokalee in Collier County, water up to 3 feet deep covered streets and roadways. In both areas, no significant damage to property was reported.Ernesto's rains also caused Fisheating Creek in Palmdale to overflow its banks. However, no significant damage was noted from the rising water due to the rural location.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm wind gust ripped off the roof of a home under construction.

Lightning started a fire at a home on Lakeview Drive but quick acting neighbors kept the home from burning to the ground. A natural gas line in the home was ruptured and was ignited by the strike but neighbors quickly turned off the gas line.

A pilot reported several waterspouts just offshore of Venice.  A trained spotter reported that one of the thin waterspouts that briefly moved onshore as a tornado before dissipating. No damage was reported.
A trained spotter reported mothball size hail.
Penny size hail was reported by the public in Port Charlotte.
A 16-year-old roofer weas struck and killed by lightning as he was reaching down for the nail gun.
Reported by trained spotter.
Emergency management reported multiple trees downed by thunderstorm wind gusts.  Two of the trees blocked Ehren Cutoff Road.
A local radio station relayed a public report of quarter size hail.
The wind gust was measured at the Southwest Florida International Airport.
An F0 tornado briefly touched down in the Rocky Point area near Stuart damaging solar panels and roofs.

Both the Okeechobee County Sheriffs office and the St. Lucie County Emergency Management simultaneously reported a small tornado on the ground just west of the Okeechobee - St. Lucie county line. No significant damage was found.

Lightning sparked a fire that completely destroyed a 6,000 square feet winery warehouse. About 27,000 wine bottles with a value of $500,000 and stored in a large walk-in cooler were destroyed. Heavy machinery and a wine tasting area were also destroyed.
Lightning sparked a fire that destroyed a 12 unit apartment complex on Manatee Avenue West.

A 58-year-old man was struck by lightning while fishing near 4th Street North in Saint Petersburg, just south of the Howard Frankland Bridge. Fire Rescue resuscitated the man before the ambulance arrived. He was then taken to the hospital and was listed in serious condition.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Lightning struck the chimney of a home and sparked a fire causing $100,000 in damage.  The home was located at the 800 block of Hawks Next Court.  The persons  inside were safely evacuated. The fire from the chimney moved through the rear exterior wall of the structure and into the attic. Two upstair rooms were damaged.

Spotters reported wind damaged that destroyed the roofs on several sheds south of Lake City along Highway 41 S toward Ellisville. Widespread reports of heavy rainfall and road flooding were also received.
Spotter measured almost 2 inches of rainfall in 30 minutes. He also reported that roads were under water near the Interstate 75 and Highway 47 intersection SW of Lake City.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 53-year-old Fort Lauderdale man drowned when he was caught in a rip current in Lauderdale-by-the-Sea.  The man tried to swim to shore but exhausted himself. There were no lifeguards at the beach.Winds were east at around 15 mph when the drowning occurred, and the Hazardous Weather Outlook issued that morning indicated a slight risk of rip currents along the Atlantic beaches. This followed several cloudy and rainy days in South Florida.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Law enforcement reported trees down near Dowling Park.
Nickel size hail was reported on Highway 200 and 27th Avenue.
A horse barn and portion of a roof were damage from thunderstorm winds. Time was estimated based on radar.
Downtown Hilliard had nickel and pea size hail. Wind gusts were estimated at 45 mph.
Heavy rainfall of 1.75 inches fell in 30 minutes. Some county dirt roads and farm fields were severely eroded.
The post office reported trees down along with quarter size hail. The trees were down in the River Bend Estates area.
Quarter size hail and trees were down along County Road 239A SW.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two reports of hail were received from the general public near the intersection of McGregor Boulevard and College Parkway.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The tornadic cell moved into southern Union county near Worthington Springs. The first damage report stated that an outbuilding was damaged, a home porch was destroyed, a car was destroyed because of a fallen tree and trees were on power lines about 10 miles W of Lake Butler. At 1920 LDT, law enforcement reported viewing a tornado at County Road 18 and 239. The cell tracked NNE toward Providence.
Several trees were down north of U.S. 90 in Glen St. Mary.
A tree fell on a home. Time was approximate.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter reported several oak tree limbs downed by thunderstorm winds.

The center of Tropical Storm Ernesto moved north across the southeast half of Florida during the day. The west side of the eye wall produced 4 to 6 inches of rain from the southwest to the northeast part of the county. Sustained winds and wind gusts remained below tropical storm force in Highlands County.

Two boys, ages 10 and 12, were struck by lightning while playing soccer in an open field at Northwest 2nd Avenue and 6th Street.  The 12-year-old was treated at a local hospital and released soon afterwards. The younger boy was in intensive care but apparently survived the strike.Witnesses said it was raining and thundering when the boys were outside playing.
A 50-year-old man drowned after getting caught in a rip current at a beach near 4050 N. Ocean Drive in Lauderdale-by-the-Sea.  The man, a tourist from Iowa, was swimming at an unguarded beach.

A 36-year-old resident of Pompano Beach drowned after getting caught in a rip current off John U. Lloyd State Park in Hollywood.The beach did not have a lifeguard present, but signs were posted at the park entrance warning of rip currents.

A 40-year-old man drowned after getting caught in a rip current while surfing near Franklin Street and Surf Road.  This was the third rip current drowning in 3 days at Broward County beaches, during a period of sustained east winds of 15-20 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Flash flooding from very heavy rains associated with Tropical Storm Ernesto affected inland portions of South Florida on August 30th. Hardest hit was the Palmdale area in Glades County where an estimated 3 to 5 inches of water entered at least 13 homes, necessitating the evacuation of residents. In Immokalee in Collier County, water up to 3 feet deep covered streets and roadways. In both areas, no significant damage to property was reported.Ernesto's rains also caused Fisheating Creek in Palmdale to overflow its banks. However, no significant damage was noted from the rising water due to the rural location.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lightning sparked a house fire on Night Heron Way in the Panther Ridge subdivision. Firefighters had to battle the roof blaze from the ground because lightning in the area would have made it too dangerous to use extension ladders.
Lightning caused a fire in the attic of a home on the 1900 block of 85th Court NW.  Firefighters took 25 minutes to bring the blaze under control.
A 31-year-old male swimming at a Treasure Island beach was pulled under by a rip current and was found 300 feet offshore.
A Gibsonia resident was injured by lightning while standing under a tree during a thunderstorm.
One inch hail was reported at the YMCA at East Lake and in the city of Palm Harbor.
A trained spotter reported nickel size hail.

The school principal at Golden Gate Elementary School was struck by lightning as he was dismissing students at the school. He was treated at the scene and released. It was raining heavily and the principal was holding an umbrella when struck.
A lightning strike caused a fire that burned down a barn near the intersection of Indiana Avenue and Tarpon Springs Road. No animals were injured.
Off-duty NWS personnel recorded 2.30 inches of rain in 40 minutes...between 220 PM and 3 PM.
Hail up to one inch and 60 mph winds reported in East Naples. Traffic signals were out in the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Nickel size hail was reported by a citizen to the broadcast media. CBS news meteorologist relayed the reported to NWS.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lightning sparked a fire that damaged 12 units in a three-story condominium building in Lely.  Several of the units were damaged enough so that families were displaced and it is estimated that it could take up to 12 months for the repairs to be completed.  One person was taken to the hospital and treated for smoke inhalation.Damage to the complex was estimated at "at least $500,000" according to East Naples Fire Department officials.
Thunderstorm winds blew down trees across US Highway 1 near Scottsmoor.

A seventeen year old male was playing on his computer when lightning evidently ran onto the mouse he was using.  The strike knocked him to the floor.  He was transported to the hospital and was released the next day.  The bolt probably ran to the computer from a phone line connection and to the mouse he was holding.
An Air Force wind tower recorded a gust to 64 MPH.
An Air Force wind tower recorded a gust to 73 MPH.
An Air Force wind tower recorded a gust to 69 MPH.
A large pole barn was blown down at a park north of Melbourne.
Sanford airport recorded a thunderstorm wind gust to 58 MPH.
Thunderstorm winds blew down a few trees in De Land.
Thunderstorm winds blew down trees in De Land.
A 31-year-old male was pulled away from shore and drowned off of Treasure Island's main beach.  He was described as a strong swimmer.
Lightning ignited a fire that destroyed a one story home on the 1500 block of 49th Terrace.  The home owners were on vacation.

Lightning struck a 16-year-old male as he was doing yard work for his family's lawn care business. The bolt struck a large nearby tree and the boy at the same time.  A large branch from the tree fell and struck the boy who was listed in critical condition at a nearby hospital.
A lightning strike caused a fire at the Arbor Apartments on Plantation Pine Lane.  Firefighters found flames and smoke coming from the roof of a three-story, 18-unit apartment building.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Spotter reported 4inches of standing water in low-lying areas near the intersection of Highway 207 and Highway 312.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Lafayette County Sheriff reported several trees down.
Several downed trees reported by the Taylor County Sheriff.
A tin roof on a shed was blown off.  Numerous trees were down.  Reported by the Taylor County Sheriff.
A tin roof on a shed was blown off.  Numerous trees were down near Cherry Lake Circle.  Reported by a SKYWARN storm spotter.
The public reported nickel size hail six miles west of Chiefland at Manatee State Park.
A SKYWARN storm spotter reported golfball size hail on Interstate 10 near mile marker 139.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A brief tornado touchdown south of Starke was observed by a patrol man. It touched down along Meng Dairy Road and downed a few trees before dissipating. Path width/length estimated, and time approximate based on radar. No circulation was evident on radar.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The public reported penny size hail.
A few downed trees reported by the Washington County EMA.
The Gulf County EMA reported penny size hail.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Emergency Management relayed the following rainfall reports from throughout the county. Rainfall was from Tropical Storm Ernesto. Amount/Location/2.50"/Flagler EOC in Bunnell2.50"/North Seminole Woods3.25"/Quail Hollow Z Section1.00"/Daytona North3.00"/CR 65 West County2.60"/Palm Coast Fire Station 211.64"/Flagler Beach2.25"/Flagler Airport2.50"/Ag Center in Espanola1.00"/Cody's Corner1.75"/Flagler Beach Station 112.60"/South Seminole Woods2.12"/Water Plant Utility Drive
EM relayed a report from a rescue unit of a funnel cloud that briefly formed over Flagler Beach then dissipated as the parent cell moved offshore.
Local TV meteorologist reported 4.6 inches of rainfall within 4 hours at the intersection of 9A and Gate Parkway.
Lightning struck a transformer which caused a brief brush fire near CR13 A.
The KJAX ASOS measured 4.22 inches of rain in one hour.
EM relayed a report of 3.25 inches of rainfall thus far in the day.

Numerous public report of a funnel cloud/water spout were received from the public. The cloud developed over the intercoastal waterway near San Pablo and J.T.B. when two outflows (from west and east) merged with the east coast sea breeze front. the circulation never touched down over land.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A utility company official reported several trees down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Quarter size was reported at Brett's Waterway Restaurant. At the same time, pea size hail was reported in downtown Fernandina Beach.

Emergency management reported 3 cars were stalled in flood waters on the 2800 block of South Pine Road in Ocala.  Flooding was ongoing at the time the report was relayed to the National Weather Service.  Doppler radar indicated that 4 to 8 inches of rainfall occurred across south and east Ocala.

The Marion County Sheriff's Office relayed a funnel cloud report from a motorist traveling between Ocala and Belleview along County Road 464 and Alternate Highway 41.  Radar showed no signs of rotation.
The public reported large tree limbs and power lines down near Daytona North.
A large tree was blown down along Vernon Ave. in Crescent City.
Strong winds blew down large tree in Palatka. Time given is the time the report was relayed to the county 911 center.
The public report two trees were blown down on a car port in the Baymeadows area.
Duval county 911 reported multiple downed power lines across the Jacksonville Westside.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trained spotter reported a funnel cloud in East Naples. Spotter also estimated wind gusts of 35 to 40 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large tree was blown down onto a home and injured a resident inside.  The tree was blown down in the Beauclerc area.
Numerous power lines were blown down aross Callahan.
Power lines were blown down in Fernandina Beach.
The public measured 2.11 inches of rain in 36 minutes.
Street flooding was reported at the corner of St. Johns and King Street. Vehicles were able to cautiously pass through the intersection where 10-12 inches of stading water was reported.
The Guana River State Park forest ranger reported trees were blown down across a trail in the park.
A weak tornado downed trees and damaged tents near adventure landing on Beach Blvd.
Quarter size hail was reported along with excessive lightning.
A spotter measured a 55 mph wind gust and reported trees down near the Columbia-Suwannee County line. A shed was damaged.

A tornadic waterspout formed over Lake Stella and dissipated before making landfall. When the cell moved over Crescent Lake, the tornado formed again and tracked east southeast toward the Flagler County coast. The tornado dissipated again before making landfall in Flagler County.
A funnel cloud was reported over Crescent Lake.
A downburst leveled corn crop fields. The farm with damage was located at the intersection of State Road 13A and Highway 305.
Thunderstorm winds downed power lines.
Duval County Parks and Recreation reported several trees and widespread limb damage from Talbot Island to Fort Caroline National Monument.

Tropical Storm "Alberto" Tropical Storm Alberto made landfall along the northeast Gulf of Mexico coast and as a weakening system move north-northeast across north Florida and southeast Georgia. This motion positioned the area on the north and east side of the storm. Northeast Florida experienced periods of sustained tropical storm force winds and gusts. Heavy rainfall occurred across the Suwannee River Valley. The lowest north Florida mean sea level pressure was 1006.1 mb measured in Gainesville as the center move north and east of the area on June 13th around 12Z.  The lowest mean sea level readings across north Florida ranged from 1006.1 mb in Gainesville to 1009.4 mb at the National Ocean System (NOS) tide gauge at NAS Mayport, Florida. Maximum sustained winds recorded were 40 knots/46 mph at Craig Airfield in east Jacksonville on June 13th around 21:11Z.  The peak gust was 47 knots/54 mph recorded at Cecil Field in west Jacksonville, on June 13th around 1950Z.  Inland stations generally reported sustained winds of 17-40 knots (20-46 mph) with gusts ranging from 30-47 knots (35-54 mph).  Many trees were downed and some structural damage occurred over the Suwannee River Valley. Scattered trees and power lines were blown down across the remainder of northeast Florida. Most damaged occurred from winds from midday July 13th into the early evening hours. Wave heights reached 6-9 ft across the coastal waters.  The maximum height observed was at the St. Augustine buoy 41012 on June 13th around 1920Z.  Tides were running 0.5-1 ft below astronomical tide predictions on the 13th. Rainfall totals across Florida ranged from 2 to 6 inches. The heaviest rainfall 5.60 inches in Hedges, Florida (Nassau County). Some minor temporary flooding of flood prone occurred, but the rainfall was beneficial to the area helping it recover from a minor drought period.  River flooding was not a concern. Two confirmed tornado touchdowns occurred, one near Jacksonville Beach with minor damage done to a water park around 08Z on June 13th.  No fatalities nor injuries were reported in association with T.S. Alberto.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 38-year-old Miami man died when lightning struck him as he was riding his ATV (all-terrain vehicle) in the Everglades about 10 miles west of Highway 27 near the Broward/Palm Beach County line. His 16-year-old daughter was riding was him and was injured, although she survived the strike. The man and his daughter were headed back towards shelter several miles away as a thunderstorm approached the area, when the lightning struck the ATV.
A South Florida Water Management weather observation platform near I-75 in far western Broward County measured a wind gust of 53 knots...61 mph during a thunderstorm.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Quarter size hail was reported along 52nd Place in Bell.
Large tree limbs were blown down at the Division of Foresty site in Lake City.

A ten year old girl was struck by lightning while on a horse farm along County Highway 42 in southeast Marion County. Lightning struck a tree and then the charge traveled along a metal fence, which the girl was holding with her hands. The girl had bruises consistent with an indirect lightning strike with red marks on her arms and abdomen and a black mark on her buttocks, which indicates a lightning exit wound. She was taken by helicopter to Orlando Regional Medical Center as a precaution.
Power lines were blown down along south Pine Avenue along the Ocala City Limits.
Thunderstorm winds damaged power lines in Flemington along State Road 318.  Clay Electric crews had to repair the lines the following day.
A funnel cloud was sighted near Highway 207 in Flagler Estates.
A 27 year old male was caught in a rip current and drowned at Matanzas Beach.  An 8 year old boy was caught in the rip current with the man, but lifeguards were able to save the boy.

The center of Tropical Storm Alberto made landfall near Adams Beach along the Taylor County coast, about 50 miles southeast of Tallahassee, Florida, near midday on June 13.  The highest 2-minute wind measured was 36 knots at Keaton Beach around 3 pm EDT on June 13.  A peak wind gust of 48 knots was recorded from Gulf Buoy 42036, about 80 miles southeast of Apalachicola.  It also recorded the lowest sea level pressure,  995.2 millibars.  Rainfall totals for the 48-hour period ending at 8 am EDT on June 14 ranged from 0.26 inches at Marianna to 7.90 inches at Ochlockonee Bay.  Maximum storm tides from Alberto varied from 1-2 feet above MLLW along the Bay and Gulf County coasts to 8-9 feet above MLLW along the Taylor and Dixie County coasts.  Maximum storm surges ranged from 3-5 feet above normal along the Wakulla, Taylor and Dixie County coasts.  Alberto's winds knocked down several trees and power lines throughout portions of the Florida Big Bend and resulted in scattered power outages.  Minor surge flooding occurred in coastal communities, especially in Taylor and Dixie counties.  About 350 coastal residents of Franklin, Wakulla, Taylor and Dixie counties took refuge in emergency shelters.  Reports were provided by the Florida DEM.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Gulf County Sheriff reported several trees and power lines down.
Quarter inch hail reported by WJHG-TV in Panama City.
Penny size hail reported along U.S. Highway 231 by the public.
Quarter size hail reported by WJHG-TV in Panama City.
ASOS measured a peak wind gust of 71 mph at the Panama City-Bay County Airport.
Lightning started a fire that caused $100,000 in damage to a home in Viera.
A 47 year old man drowned in a rip current at Daytona Beach.

Pahokee police reported downed power lines along Hoover Dike west of Pahokee on the eastern shore of Lake Okeechobee. A South Florida Water Management observation platform recorded a wind gust to 44 knots...51 mph...at 345 PM EST.

Lightning struck just outside of a home in Lauderhill during the early evening hours, causing a large hole in the driveway, knocking a garage door off its hinges and damaging the electrical panel in a car in front of the garage.The lightning was related to a band of thunderstorms loosely associated with Tropical Storm Ernesto which moved across South Florida 2 days prior.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Lightning strikes caused two fires. Report from the Division of Forestry.
EMA reported average wave heights of 6 feet with some 8 foot breakers along Flagler Beach. Sea spray was noted along A1A.
Spotter measured 1.22 inches of rainfall within 20 minutes.
Rainbow Lakes State Park reported a crushed pavilion due to a large downed oak tree. The tree was leveled from strong thunderstorm wind gusts. Time is estimated based on radar.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A group of recruits were attending Army Ranger Camp and were laying on the ground as a thunderstorm  approached them.  Evidently, lightning hit a nearby tree and ran through the root system of the tree.  Six of the rangers were injured by the strike and were transported to a local hospital and later released.  None of them suffered any serious injuries.
A tornado was reported on the ground by the general public. Power lines were snapped by the torndo but no significant damage was reported. Nickel size hail was also reported.
Rainfall of 5.39 inches was reported in one hour. No significant flooding or damage was reported.

Lightning struck a tree and killed a 13-year-old boy who was standing nearby.  The boy was among a group of kids playing at a popular swimming hole at Ocean Park Drive.  It was not raining at the time of the strike.
Lightning started a fire that consumed 70 percent of a home on the 13800 block of Oak Forest Blvd S.
Numerous reports of dime to quarter size hail were reported by the general public.
Penny size hail was reported by a SKYWARN storm spotter.
A 57 year old woman drowned in a rip current at Daytona Beach Shores.
County workers photographed an F0 tornado on the ground just north of Deer Park. No significant damage was noted.
County workers watched an F0 tornado touch down in a wooded area just southwest of Holopaw.  No other damage was noted.
Thunderstorm winds blew down large tents and advertising signs at the St. Lucie County faregrounds.
Lightning struck a home on the east side of Panama City Beach and caused extensive damage.  Reported by the Panama City News Herald.
A 64 year old man drowned in a rip current in Daytona Beach Shores.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An off duty NWS employee reported a waterspout over the St. Johns River near Mayport. Several other reports were received by the general public and TV meteorologists.  A SPECI report from Craig Airfield reported a waterspout at 1759 local time, located NW of the airfield about 7 miles away.  The waterspout dissipated before moving over land.
A pilot at Craig Field reported a funnel cloud to the NW.
Spotter measured 2.78 inches of rainfall in the Courtyards Subdivision on Mayport Road.
Spotter measured a storm total rainfall amount of 5.62 inches.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: High winds from a thunderstorm blew down several trees between Molino and Cantonment.

Four mobile homes suffered minor damage from strong downburst winds during a thunderstorm at Naples Mobile Home Estates in East Naples.  Most of the damage was done to carports, windows and screens. Damage survey done by Collier County Emergency Management Agency officials determined the source of the winds to be from a downburst and not tornado-related.

Heavy rains from thunderstorms caused the roof of a home in Opa-Locka to cave in. The dining room and a bedroom were destroyed, and extensive water damage was observed.The roof had recently undergone repairs from Hurricane Wilma damage in October 2005.
Wind gust 51 knots (59 mph) measured at Duck Key in association with a rain band from Tropical Storm Ernesto.

Tropical Storm Ernesto tracked from the Florida Straits, making landfall in Islamorada late in the evening on August 29, 2006.  Ernesto continued north northwest through Florida Bay before reaching mainland Monroe County on the morning of August 30.  62 residents were sheltered within the county, with 35 special needs residents sheltered out-of-county. In the Upper Keys of Monroe County, peak wind gusts included 42 knots (48 mph) at Molasses Reef Light, and 41 knots (47 mph) at the Islamorada Fire/Rescue Station.  Small tree limbs were reported down along county road 905, and one tree was uprooted in the Lake Surprise subdivision.  Lowest pressure reported was 1004.1 mb at Molasses Reef Light.  Storm total rainfall was measured at 2.50 inches at Tavernier, and 2.32 inches at John Pennekamp State Park.  No significant storm tide was measured or reported.

Tropical Storm Ernesto tracked from the Florida Straits, making landfall in Islamorada late in the evening on August 29, 2006.  Ernesto continued north northwest through Florida Bay before reaching mainland Monroe County on the morning of August 30.  62 residents were sheltered within the county, with 35 special needs residents sheltered out-of-county.  In the Lower Keys of Monroe County, maximum wind gusts were measured at 35 knots (40 mph) at Sand Key Light and 34 knots (39 mph) and Key West International Airport. Minimum pressure recorded was 1008.1 mb at Key West Airport.  Storm total rainfall totaled around 2 inches at Cudjoe Key, and 4.59 inches at Big Pine Key, and 1.35 inches in Key West.No significant storm tide was observed in the Lower Keys.  Rainfall caused minor street flooding on Big Pine Key the morning of August 30th.  County damage of 200 dollars was due to equipment destroyed by a leaking roof.

Tropical Storm Ernesto tracked from the Florida Straits, making landfall in Islamorada late in the evening on August 29, 2006.  Ernesto continued north northwest through Florida Bay before reaching mainland Monroe County on the morning of August 30.  62 residents were sheltered within the county, with 35 special needs residents sheltered out-of-county. In the Middle Keys of Monroe County, peak wind gusts were measured at 52 knots (60 mph) from a rain band at Long Key CMAN station, and 51 knots (59 mph) at Duck Key during the early afternoon of August 29th.  A peak wind gust was measured at 38 knots (44 mph) at Sombrero Key Light. Small tree limbs were down in Key Colony Beach.  The minimum pressure recorded was 1005.4 mb at Long Key Light CMAN.  Storm total rainfall included 3.18 inches at Marathon, 3.91 inches at Curry Hammock State Park, and 3.63 inches at Duck Key.  Storm tides reached 1.5 feet above sea level between 0500 and 0600 EST on August 30th at Vaca Key (Marathon), but no flooding was reported.
Lightning struck a home in the Crestview area.  The strike started a fire and heavily damaged the home.  No one was home at the time of the strike.
A twenty-five year old female was talking on the telephone when lightning struck.  She was taken to the hospital and was later released with minor injuries.
High winds from a thunderstorm blew down several trees and damaged some farm machinery just south of Jay.

A few funnel clouds were observed with a strong thunderstorm which moved north at 10 mph. The funnel clouds were over uninhabited areas from near the Loxahatchee National Wildlife Refuge to just south of Wellington.

Very heavy rains from Tropical Storm Ernesto caused the Fisheating Creek at Palmdale to overflow its banks. The creek reached a maximum stage of 8.4 feet at 4 PM EST on 9/2/06. Flood stage is 7.0 feet. Fisheating creek remained above flood stage until 9/7/06.Flooding was reported at the campground, including the restrooms and the store.  No significant damage was noted.
Trained spotter reported quarter size hail in Lake Worth.  Thunderstorms in the area also knocked out power to about 26,000 customers for up to two and a half hours.
A few downed trees along State Highway 79 reported by the Holmes County Sheriff.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Tropical Storm Ernesto moved across the Straits of Florida during the morning and early afternoon hours of August 29th, making landfall in the Upper Florida Keys around sunset, with a second landfall over far southwestern Miami-Dade County around midnight on the 30th. Ernesto moved north-northwest over the Everglades of South Florida during the overnight and morning hours of the 30th, then turned north and northeast over eastern sections of Hendry County during the midday, and passing over western portions of Lake Okeechobee during the early afternoon hours of the 30th. Ernesto is characterized as a minor tropical storm with little overall impact on South Florida, and no direct casualties were noted. There were two indirect deaths in Miami-Dade and Broward counties resulting from vehicle accidents.Maximum observed sustained winds were 42 knots (48 mph) at the C-MAN station in Fowey Rocks, with a peak gust of 50 knots (58 mph).  Due to the higher than standard anemometer height at Fowey Rocks, a reduction to standard 10-meter elevation yielded a highest sustained wind of 37 knots (43 mph).  Tropical storm force winds were confined to coastal sections along the Atlantic coast, as well as over Lake Okeechobee where South Florida Water Management District platforms LZ40 and L005 recorded maximum sustained winds of 35 and 34 knots (40 and 39 mph) respectively. Platform L005 recorded a peak gust of 51 knots (59 mph). The lowest sea-level pressure observed was 1004 mb in Opa-Locka, although estimated lowest pressures on Lake Okeechobee were 1003 mb. Coastal storm tides of around 1 foot above astronomical tide levels were observed on both Atlantic and Gulf coasts, with no beach erosion reported. Rainfall was heaviest over interior sections of South Florida, primarily over the Big Cypress National Preserve of Collier County where 48-hour storm total amounts ranged between 7 and 8 inches. Other rainfall amounts in excess of 5 inches were observed over the rest of interior South Florida including the areas around Lake Okeechobee. These rains caused the Fisheating Creek to overflow its banks at Palmdale in Glades County, and also resulted in some evacuation of homes. Flooding also occurred in portions of Collier County, especially near Golden Gate Estates and Immokalee, with no damage to structures reported.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Wakulla County Sheriff reported several trees and power lines down along Spring Creek Highway.
High winds from a thunderstorm blew down several trees near Beulah.

Off-duty NWS meteorologist measured a 60 mph (52 knot) wind gust during a thunderstorm. Large Maleleuca tree branches were broken off trees near I-75 and Miami Gardens Drive.  Half-inch hail was also reported with this storm.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Wind event along coast.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: High winds from a thunderstorm blew down trees and power lines just west of Wright.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Same as Alabama.

The tides were high enough to flood several of the roads along Santa Rosa Island, especially near Fort Pickens and Navarre Beach.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak tornado moved onshore Golden Beach and dissipated as it reached the dune line at the beach.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A waterspout moved onshore Golden Beach as a very weak F0 tornado. The event was captured by news media helicopters as the waterspout moved onshore. The tornado moved onto the beach about 50 yards before lifting at the dune line. No damage was observed or reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A small-scale area of low pressure, referred to as a mesolow, formed over the Gulf of Mexico along a cold front which was moving into central Florida. The center of this feature was accompanied by a small band of showers and lightning storms as it moved onshore near the mouth of Tampa Bay. These storms moved rapidly northeast at 35 to 40 mph crossing the Kissimmee River into northwestern Osceola County. With the mesolow increasing, significant rotation was noted on doppler radar as the storms moved through northwestern Orange County and then into Seminole County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An F0 tornado touched down in Orlando just east of Lake Baldwin damaging roofs and windows of 5 apartment buildings. The tornado skipped east about a mile into Winter Park, damaging roofs on another apartment and three houses. A large cab-over truck was overturned.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A small-scale area of low pressure, referred to as a mesolow, formed over the Gulf of Mexico along a cold front which was moving into central Florida. The center of this feature was accompanied by a small band of showers and lightning storms as it moved onshore near the mouth of Tampa Bay. These storms moved rapidly northeast at 35 to 40 mph crossing the Kissimmee River into northwestern Osceola County. With the mesolow increasing, significant rotation was noted on doppler radar as the storms moved through northwestern Orange County and then into Seminole County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An F1 tornado touched down in southern Seminole County just north of Aloma Avenue and moved northeast, crossing the 417 Beltway and lifting along West Chapman Road. The tornado severly damaged four homes. Eight homes had moderate damage and 32 homes and mobile homes had minor damage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front and squall line moved through the region on 16 November with some lightning and non-thunderstorm wind damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lightning struck a tree near southwest 25th place in Ocala. The lightning then traveled through the ground to a propane tank in the immediate vicinity of a residence. The propane tank exploded causing minor damage to the kitchen of the home.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong high pressure center built to the north of the region with strong cold air damming east of the Appalachians.  At this time a cut off upper level low moved east out of the lower Mississippi River Valley with (for the local area) strong extratropical cyclogenesis off the Georgia and South Carolina Coast.  With these conditions set up a cold air moved over coastal areas of southeast Georgia and northeast Florida. By Tuesday night, 11/21/2006, some snow and sleet was reported mixing in with the rain.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow and sleet occurred in Flagler County between 07:30 and 09:30 local time. The winter weather occurred at Flagler Beach, North Palm Coast, and Flagler County Airport. The winter weather was mixed with drizzle at times.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A vigorous short wave trough and squall line moved across north Florida on Christmas Day resulting in severe weather across the northern and central portions of the state and a tornado near Lake City, Columbia County, Florida.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous trees were blown down across power lines. Power outages were reported starting at 07:43 EST due to downed trees on power lines and continued through 08:14 EST. A total of 179 customers were affected across Suwannee County.  Report courtesy of the Suwannee Valley Electric Cooperative.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy rainfall fell across portions of north Florida.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rainfall between 1.76 inches and 5 inches fell across the county. Some significant rainfall reports are, 1.76 inches at Flagler Beach, 2.05 inches at Palm Harbor in Palm Coast, 2.50 inches at South Seminole Woods in Palm Coast, 3.00 inches in Daytona North and North Seminole Woods in Palm Coast, 3.26 inches at Water Plant # 2 in Seminole Woods, 3.49 inches at Pine Lakes, 3.67 inches at Flagler County Airport, 3.75 inches at Haw Creek, 3.90 inches at north Palm Coast, 4.10 inches at north Indian Trails, 4.18 inches at the Flagler Emergency Operations Center in Bunnell, 4.30 inches in northeast Palm Coast, 4.30 inches at Water Plant # 1 in Palm Coast, 4.50 inches at Pine Lakes in Palm Coast, 4.90 inches at south Indian Trails, and 5.00 inches in the City of Bunnell.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A vigorous short wave trough and squall line moved across north Florida on Christmas Day resulting in severe weather across the northern and central portions of the state and a tornado near Lake City, Columbia County, Florida.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A large tree fell on a house with Jacksonville Fire/Rescue called out to remove it.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A vigorous short wave trough and squall line moved across north Florida on Christmas Day resulting in severe weather across the northern and central portions of the state and a tornado near Lake City, Columbia County, Florida.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Columbia County Emergency Management reports damage to 12 homes and one home destroyed. A business was also destroyed. Extensive damage was reported in the community of Woodborough along Scenic Lake drive as well as along Moore road northwest of Lake City. One minor injury was reported with minor lacerations.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A squall line produced several reports of wind damage as well as hail during the afternoon of the 15th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several trees down on State Highway 85 near the Walton-Okaloosa County line.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A squall line produced several reports of wind damage as well as hail during the afternoon of the 15th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous trees down in and around Pittman.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A squall line produced several reports of wind damage as well as hail during the afternoon of the 15th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Nickel to quarter size hail reported in Graceville.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A squall line produced several reports of wind damage as well as hail during the afternoon of the 15th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Some downed trees and power lines reported in and around Vernon.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A squall line produced several reports of wind damage as well as hail during the afternoon of the 15th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Straight-line winds downed several trees and utility poles.  Approximately 1,000 customers were without power.  A beach flagploe was blown over on U.S. Highway 98.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The squall line which brought tornadoes, torrential rain and damaging winds to southeast Alabama and the Florida Panhandle weakened as it moved east into the Big Bend.  However, a few of the thunderstorms still produced severe weather.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Straight-line winds destroyed the roof of a house located north of Adams Beach.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A squall line produced several reports of wind damage as well as hail during the afternoon of the 15th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Straight-line winds downed several trees.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A squall line produced several reports of wind damage as well as hail during the afternoon of the 15th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several downed trees in Ponce de Leon reported by a county 911 dispatcher.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A squall line produced several reports of wind damage as well as hail during the afternoon of the 15th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several downed trees in Bonifay reported by a county 911 dispatcher.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A squall line produced several reports of wind damage as well as hail during the afternoon of the 15th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Straight-line winds damaged a home and barn and destroyed a fence on County Road 181.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A state of local emergency now exists in Union County, Florida due to the severe threat of fire to lives and property for the residents of Union County, Florida; and that due to the official drought index and known dry and windy conditions, which continue to cause, exacerbate and increase the threat of wildfires within Union County, further measures are required to protect Union County citizens and visitors and to other wise protect their health, safety, welfare and property.  Therefore a burn ban has been placed in effect for the next 14 days.  In Bradford County, Florida rarmers are running their irrigation systems at $200 to $300 a day to produce greens, and feed for their farm animals. They also have 1 fish camp who has suffered a loss due to the drought. Bradford county farmers have lost approx. $30,000.00 so far this year, and the fish camp has lost $2,000.00 this year for a total of 32,000.00.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: In Bradford County, Florida rarmers are running their irrigation systems at $200 to $300 a day to produce greens, and feed for their farm animals. They also have 1 fish camp who has suffered a loss due to the drought. Bradford county farmers have lost approx. $30,000.00 so far this year, and the fish camp has lost $2,000.00 this year for a total of 32,000.00.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow-moving low pressure trough caused very heavy rains and flooding over portions of Palm Beach County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A slow-moving low pressure trough caused very heavy rains and significant flooding over parts of Palm Beach County. West Palm Beach International Airport received a total of 8.21 inches of rain ending at 7 PM on the 15th. Other locations in Central and Southern Palm Beach County received between 6 and 8 inches of rain. Northern Broward County received lesser amounts in the 2 to 3 inch range.|Several streets and roads were closed in the city of West Palm Beach, with water reaching up to 3 feet deep in some areas. Hardest hit was the neighborhood of Pineapple Park. Many vehicles were stranded in the deep water, with local police receiving about 120 calls for assistance. No significant damage was reported to property despite water entering homes and businesses. |Florida Power and Light reported 20,000 customers without power during the afternoon and early evening hours. Shelters were opened for people left homeless by the floods, but only 5 people had arrived as of 8:30 PM.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong rip currents were observed along southeast Florida beaches over New Years weekend.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 62-year-old man drowned after getting caught in a rip current at an unguarded beach in the 3400 block of Galt Ocean Drive on Fort Lauderdale Beach. The man was a part-time resident of South Florida who also had a residence in New York.|Southeast Florida Ocean Rescue agencies performed dozens of rescues over New Years weekend as a result of the strong rip currents observed along the Atlantic beaches. Several of the rescued swimmers required medical attention.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Dense fog formed over interior sections of South Florida during the early morning hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Dense fog formed over interior sections of South Florida during the early morning hours. This caused an accident involving six vehicles on Immokalee Road in interior Northern portions of Collier County.|One person, a 38-year-old man, was killed when his pickup truck collided head-on with a Collier Area Transit Bus. This then caused a six-vehicle pileup on Immokalee Road. Three other people sustained minor injuries.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of thunderstorms ahead of a cold front moved across the eastern Gulf of Mexico and through much of Florida. Doppler radar showed bow echoes with several of the storms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A utility company storm survey in Webster showed an unanchored and unused mobile home flipped and totally destroyed with the debris field extending in a straight line to the northeast. Large tree limbs brought down power lines in the area.  Emergency Management also reported an older mobile home with one third of the home destroyed near Center Hill. At least 100 trees were downed during this downburst event.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy rains moving through Southeast Florida caused damage to apartment complexes in Kendall and Miramar, as well as gusty winds in excess of 35 knots.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rains swept across South Florida during the very early morning hours and caused the ceiling of an apartment to collapse in eastern sections of Miramar. A seven-year-old boy received minor injured when the ceiling collapsed on him. The rest of his family, three adults and another child, were not injured.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy rains moving through Southeast Florida caused damage to apartment complexes in Kendall and Miramar, as well as gusty winds in excess of 35 knots.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rains which swept across the area during the very early morning hours caused the flooding of 102 units in the Cherry Grove Village apartments in Kendall. |The buildings were being refurbished and water entered from balconies into  uncovered window openings. Nearly 200 residents were displaced.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of thunderstorms ahead of a cold front moved across the eastern Gulf of Mexico and through much of Florida. Doppler radar showed bow echoes with several of the storms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A downburst wind gust produced downed trees and light damage to homes north of State Road 52 and east of the damage produced by the brief tornado in the Tampa Golf and Country Club.

Therefore a burn ban has been placed in effect for the next 14 days.  In Bradford County, Florida rarmers are running their irrigation systems at $200 to $300 a day to produce greens, and feed for their farm animals. They also have 1 fish camp who has suffered a loss due to the drought. Bradford county farmers have lost approx. $30,000.00 so far this year, and the fish camp has lost $2,000.00 this year for a total of 32,000.00.
